Now let's think about how JavaScript actually works here.
First, the global execution environment gets created — that's
The syntax parser starts reading through our code, character by character: f-u-n-c-t... (parsing one char at a time).
The compiler starts turning the code we wrote into instructions the computer can understand.
The "global execution context" gets created, and at this point global objects like
Then we enter the creation phase (Hoisting), where our variables and functions get created.
Functions get stored in memory first, and they don't run their inner code until we actually call them. So back in the earlier example:
the functions are just sitting in memory
. It's not until execution moves down and hits the invocation we wrote —
— that it starts running the function and the code inside it. Let me pause here for a second, because when
runs, something kind of magical happens:
a new execution context gets created
. And this process is what we call the execution stack.

The execution stack is built up layer by layer, and whatever's on top is the thing currently running. So every time we call a function, we're creating a new execution context and pushing it onto the execution stack.
This new execution context is special — it has its own
, its own memory, its own functions, and so on. And it goes through the same creation and hoisting phases, then runs the code line by line, character by character.
pauses, and a new execution context gets created. From this we can see that every function creates a
new execution context and goes through the creation phase and so on
— and the order in which the code runs doesn't get messed up by the execution stack.

is left in the stack, because it still needs to create the variable

So we can see that each time you run a function, a new execution context gets created, and then the code runs synchronously line by line, character by character, until it's done.

Invocation just means calling a function. It's a really important concept, so when someone says "function invocation" or "invocation function," they just mean running that function — and we run it with the parentheses (). This post walks through what actually happens when you call a function, how a new execution context gets created each time, and how that builds up the execution stack.
